CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 2)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 2)

Review on Channel coding

Let FFF be the input alphabet, Φ\PhiΦ be the output alphabet.

e.g. F={0,1},RF=\{0,1\},\mathbb{R}F={0,1},R.

Introduce noise: Pr⁡(c′ received∣c transmitted)\operatorname{Pr}(c'\text{ received}|c\text{ transmitted})Pr(c′ received∣c transmitted).

We use uuu to denote the information to be transmitted

ccc to be the codeword.

c′c'c′ is the received codeword. given to the decoder.

u′u'u′ is the decoded information word.

Error if u′≠uu' \neq uu′=u.

Example:

Binary symmetric channel (BSC)

F=Φ={0,1}F=\Phi=\{0,1\}F=Φ={0,1}

Every bit of ccc is flipped with probability ppp.

Binary erasure channel (BEC)

F=Φ={0,1,∗}F=\Phi=\{0,1,*\}F=Φ={0,1,∗}, very common in practice when we are unsure when the bit is transmitted.

ccc is transmitted, c′c'c′ is received.

c′c'c′ is ccc with probability 1−p1-p1−p, eee with probability ppp.

Encoding

Encoding EEE is a function from FkF^kFk to FnF^nFn.

Where E(u)=cE(u)=cE(u)=c is the codeword.

Assume n≥kn\geq kn≥k, we don’t compress the information.

A code C\mathcal{C}C is a subset of FnF^nFn.

Encoding is a one to one mapping from FkF^kFk to C\mathcal{C}C.

In practice, we usually choose C⊆Fn\mathcal{C}\subseteq F^nC⊆Fn to be the size of FkF^kFk.

Decoding

DDD is a function from Φn\Phi^nΦn to C\mathcal{C}C.

D(c′)=c^D(c')=\hat{c}D(c′)=c^

The decoder then outputs the unique u′u'u′ such that E(u′)=c^E(u')=\hat{c}E(u′)=c^.

Our aim is to have u=u′u=u'u=u′.

Decoding error probability: P⁡err=max⁡c∈CP⁡err(c)\operatorname{P}_{err}=\max_{c\in \mathcal{C}}\operatorname{P}_{err}(c)Perr​=maxc∈C​Perr​(c).

where P⁡err(c)=∑y∣D(y)≠cPr⁡(y received∣c transmitted)\operatorname{P}_{err}(c)=\sum_{y|D(y)\neq c}\operatorname{Pr}(y\text{ received}|c\text{ transmitted})Perr​(c)=∑y∣D(y)=c​Pr(y received∣c transmitted).

Our goal is to construct decoder DDD such that P⁡err\operatorname{P}_{err}Perr​ is bounded.

Example:

Repetition code in binary symmetric channel:

Let F=Φ={0,1}F=\Phi=\{0,1\}F=Φ={0,1}. Every bit of ccc is flipped with probability ppp.

Say k=1k=1k=1, n=3n=3n=3 and let C={000,111}\mathcal{C}=\{000,111\}C={000,111}.

Let the encoder be E(u)=uuuE(u)=u u uE(u)=uuu.

The decoder is D(000)=D(100)=D(010)=D(001)=0D(000)=D(100)=D(010)=D(001)=0D(000)=D(100)=D(010)=D(001)=0, D(110)=D(101)=D(011)=D(111)=1D(110)=D(101)=D(011)=D(111)=1D(110)=D(101)=D(011)=D(111)=1.

Exercise: Compute the error probability of the repetition code in binary symmetric channel.

Solution

Recall that Perr(c)=∑y∣D(y)≠cPr⁡(y received∣c transmitted)P_{err}(c)=\sum_{y|D(y)\neq c}\operatorname{Pr}(y\text{ received}|c\text{ transmitted})Perr​(c)=∑y∣D(y)=c​Pr(y received∣c transmitted).

Use binomial random variable:

Perr(000)=∑y∣D(y)≠000Pr⁡(y received∣000 transmitted)=Pr⁡(2 flipes or more)=(n2)p2(1−p)+(n3)p3=3p2(1−p)+p3\begin{aligned} P_{err}(000)&=\sum_{y|D(y)\neq 000}\operatorname{Pr}(y\text{ received}|000\text{ transmitted})\\ &=\operatorname{Pr}(2\text{ flipes or more})\\ &=\binom{n}{2}p^2(1-p)+\binom{n}{3}p^3\\ &=3p^2(1-p)+p^3\\ \end{aligned}Perr​(000)​=y∣D(y)=000∑​Pr(y received∣000 transmitted)=Pr(2 flipes or more)=(2n​)p2(1−p)+(3n​)p3=3p2(1−p)+p3​

The computation is identical for 111111111.

Perr=max⁡{Perr(000),Perr(111)}=Perr(000)=3p2(1−p)+p3P_{err}=\max\{P_{err}(000),P_{err}(111)\}=P_{err}(000)=3p^2(1-p)+p^3Perr​=max{Perr​(000),Perr​(111)}=Perr​(000)=3p2(1−p)+p3.

Maximum likelihood principle

For p≤1/2p\leq 1/2p≤1/2, the last example is maximum likelihood decoder.

Notice that Pr⁡(c′=000∣c=000)=(1−p)3\operatorname{Pr}(c'=000|c=000)=(1-p)^3Pr(c′=000∣c=000)=(1−p)3 and Pr⁡(c′=000∣c=111)=p3\operatorname{Pr}(c'=000|c=111)=p^3Pr(c′=000∣c=111)=p3.

  • If p≤1/2p\leq 1/2p≤1/2, then (1−p)3≥p3(1-p)^3\geq p^3(1−p)3≥p3. c=000c=000c=000 is more likely to be transmitted than c=111c=111c=111.

When Pr⁡(c′=001∣c=000)=(1−p)2p\operatorname{Pr}(c'=001|c=000)=(1-p)^2pPr(c′=001∣c=000)=(1−p)2p and Pr⁡(c′=001∣c=111)=p2(1−p)\operatorname{Pr}(c'=001|c=111)=p^2(1-p)Pr(c′=001∣c=111)=p2(1−p).

  • If p≤1/2p\leq 1/2p≤1/2, then (1−p)2p≥p2(1−p)(1-p)^2p\geq p^2(1-p)(1−p)2p≥p2(1−p). c=001c=001c=001 is more likely to be transmitted than c=110c=110c=110.

For p>1/2p>1/2p>1/2, we just negate the above.

In general, Maximum likelihood decoder is D(c′)=arg⁡max⁡c∈CPr⁡(c′ received∣c transmitted)D(c')=\arg\max_{c\in \mathcal{C}}\operatorname{Pr}(c'\text{ received}|c\text{ transmitted})D(c′)=argmaxc∈C​Pr(c′ received∣c transmitted).

Defining a “good” code

Two metrics:

  • How many redundant bits are needed?
    • e.g. repetition code: k=1k=1k=1, n=3n=3n=3 sends 222 redundant bits.
  • What is the resulting error probability?
    • Depends on the decoding function.
    • Normally, maximum likelihood decoding is assumed.
    • Should go zero with nnn.

Definition for rate of code is kn\frac{k}{n}nk​.

More generally, log⁡∣F∣∣C∣n\log_{|F|}\frac{|\mathcal{C}|}{n}log∣F∣​n∣C∣​.

Definition for information entropy

Let XXX be a random variable over a discrete set X\mathcal{X}X.

  • That is every x∈Xx\in \mathcal{X}x∈X has a probability Pr⁡(X=x)\operatorname{Pr}(X=x)Pr(X=x).

The entropy H(X)H(X)H(X) of a discrete random variable XXX is defined as:

H(X)=Ex∼Xlog⁡1Pr⁡(x)=−∑x∈XPr⁡(x)log⁡Pr⁡(x)H(X)=\mathbb{E}_{x\sim X}{\log \frac{1}{\operatorname{Pr}(x)}}=-\sum_{x\in \mathcal{X}}\operatorname{Pr}(x)\log \operatorname{Pr}(x)H(X)=Ex∼X​logPr(x)1​=−x∈X∑​Pr(x)logPr(x)

when X=Bernouili(p)X=Bernouili(p)X=Bernouili(p), we denote H(X)=H(p)=−plog⁡p−(1−p)log⁡(1−p)H(X)=H(p)=-p\log p-(1-p)\log (1-p)H(X)=H(p)=−plogp−(1−p)log(1−p).

A deeper explanation will be given in the later in the course.

Which rate are possible?

Claude Shannon ‘48: Coding theorem of the BSC(binary symmetric channel)

Recall r=knr=\frac{k}{n}r=nk​.

Let H(⋅)H(\cdot)H(⋅) be the entropy function.

For every 0≤r<1−H(p)0\leq r<1-H(p)0≤r<1−H(p),

  • There exists C1,C2,…\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2,\ldotsC1​,C2​,… of rates r1,r2,…r_1,r_2,\ldotsr1​,r2​,… lengths n1,n2,…n_1,n_2,\ldotsn1​,n2​,… and ri≥rr_i\geq rri​≥r.
  • That with Maximum likelihood decoding satisifies Perr→0P_{err}\to 0Perr​→0 as i→∞i\to \inftyi→∞.

For any R≥1−H(p)R\geq 1-H(p)R≥1−H(p),

  • Any sequence C1,C2,…\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2,\ldotsC1​,C2​,… of rates r1,r2,…r_1,r_2,\ldotsr1​,r2​,… lengths n1,n2,…n_1,n_2,\ldotsn1​,n2​,… and ri≥Rr_i\geq Rri​≥R,
  • Any andy decoding algorithm, Perr→1P_{err}\to 1Perr​→1 as i→∞i\to \inftyi→∞.

1−H(p)1-H(p)1−H(p) is the capacity of the BSC.

  • Informally, the capacity is the best possible rate of the code (asymptotically).
  • A special case of a broader theorem (Shannon’s coding theorem).
  • We will see later in this course.

Polar codes, for explicit construction of codes with rate arbitrarily close to capacity.

BSC capacity - Intuition

Capacity of the binary symmetric channel with crossover probability p=1−H(p)p=1-H(p)p=1−H(p).

A correct decoder c′→cc'\to cc′→c essentially identifies two objects:

  • The codeword ccc
  • The error word e=c′−ce=c'-ce=c′−c subtraction mod  2\mod 2mod2.
  • ccc and eee are independent of each other.

A typical eee has ≈np\approx np≈np 111‘s (law of large numbers), say n(p±δ)n(p\pm \delta)n(p±δ).

Exercise:

Pr⁡(e)=pn(p±δ)(1−p)n(1−p±δ)=2−n(H(p)+ϵ)\operatorname{Pr}(e)=p^{n(p\pm \delta)}(1-p)^{n(1-p\pm \delta)}=2^{-n(H(p)+\epsilon)}Pr(e)=pn(p±δ)(1−p)n(1−p±δ)=2−n(H(p)+ϵ) for some ϵ\epsilonϵ goes to zero as n→∞n\to \inftyn→∞.

Intuition

There exists ≈2n(H(p)\approx 2^{n(H(p)}≈2n(H(p) typical error words.

To index those typical error words, we need log⁡2(2nH(p))=nH(p)+O(1)\log_2 (2^{nH(p)})=nH(p)+O(1)log2​(2nH(p))=nH(p)+O(1). bits to identify the error word eee.

To encode the message, we need log⁡2∣C∣\log_2 |\mathcal{C}|log2​∣C∣ bits.

Since we send nnn bits, the rate is k+nH(p)+O(1)≤nk+nH(p)+O(1)\leq nk+nH(p)+O(1)≤n, so kn≤1−H(p)\frac{k}{n}\leq 1-H(p)nk​≤1−H(p).

So the rate cannot exceed 1−H(p)1-H(p)1−H(p).

Formal proof

Pr⁡(e)=pn(p±δ)(1−p)n(1−p±δ)=pnp(1−p)n(1−p)p±nδ(1−p)∓nδ\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Pr}(e)&=p^{n(p\pm \delta)}(1-p)^{n(1-p\pm \delta)}\\ &=p^{np}(1-p)^{n(1-p)}p^{\pm n\delta}(1-p)^{\mp n\delta}\\ \end{aligned}Pr(e)​=pn(p±δ)(1−p)n(1−p±δ)=pnp(1−p)n(1−p)p±nδ(1−p)∓nδ​

And

2−n(H(p)+ϵ)=2−n(−plog⁡p−(1−p)log⁡(1−p)+ϵ)=2nplog⁡p2n(1−p)log⁡(1−p)2−nϵ=pnp(1−p)n(1−p)2−nϵ\begin{aligned} 2^{-n(H(p)+\epsilon)}&=2^{-n(-p\log p-(1-p)\log (1-p)+\epsilon)}\\ &=2^{np\log p}2^{n(1-p)\log (1-p)}2^{-n\epsilon}\\ &=p^{np}(1-p)^{n(1-p)}2^{-n\epsilon}\\ \end{aligned}2−n(H(p)+ϵ)​=2−n(−plogp−(1−p)log(1−p)+ϵ)=2nplogp2n(1−p)log(1−p)2−nϵ=pnp(1−p)n(1−p)2−nϵ​

So we need to check there exists ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0 such that

lim⁡n→∞p±nδ(1−p)∓nδ≤2−nϵ\lim_{n\to \infty}p^{\pm n\delta}(1-p)^{\mp n\delta}\leq 2^{-n\epsilon}n→∞lim​p±nδ(1−p)∓nδ≤2−nϵ

Test

2−nϵ=pnp(1−p)n(1−p)2−nϵ−nϵ=δnlog⁡p−δnlog⁡(1−p)ϵ=δ(log⁡(1−p)−log⁡p)\begin{aligned} 2^{-n\epsilon}&=p^{np}(1-p)^{n(1-p)}2^{-n\epsilon}\\ -n\epsilon&=\delta n\log p-\delta n\log (1-p)\\ \epsilon&=\delta (\log (1-p)-\log p)\\ \end{aligned}2−nϵ−nϵϵ​=pnp(1−p)n(1−p)2−nϵ=δnlogp−δnlog(1−p)=δ(log(1−p)−logp)​

Hamming distance

How to quantify the noise in the channel?

  • Number of flipped bits.

Definition of Hamming distance:

  • Denote c=(c1,c2,…,cn)c=(c_1,c_2,\ldots,c_n)c=(c1​,c2​,…,cn​) and c′=(c1′,c2′,…,cn′)c'=(c'_1,c'_2,\ldots,c'_n)c′=(c1′​,c2′​,…,cn′​).
  • dH(c,c′)=∑i=1n[ci≠ci′]d_H(c,c')=\sum_{i=1}^n [c_i\neq c'_i]dH​(c,c′)=∑i=1n​[ci​=ci′​].

Minimum hamminng distance:

  • Let C\mathcal{C}C be a code.
  • dH(C)=min⁡c1,c2∈C,c1≠c2dH(c1,c2)d_H(\mathcal{C})=\min_{c_1,c_2\in \mathcal{C},c_1\neq c_2}d_H(c_1,c_2)dH​(C)=minc1​,c2​∈C,c1​=c2​​dH​(c1​,c2​).

Hamming distance is a metric.

  • dH(x,y)≥0d_H(x,y)\geq 0dH​(x,y)≥0 equal iff x=yx=yx=y.
  • dH(x,y)=dH(y,x)d_H(x,y)=d_H(y,x)dH​(x,y)=dH​(y,x)
  • Triangle inequality: dH(x,y)≤dH(x,z)+dH(z,y)d_H(x,y)\leq d_H(x,z)+d_H(z,y)dH​(x,y)≤dH​(x,z)+dH​(z,y)

Level of error handling

  • error detection
  • erasure correction
  • error correction

Erasure: replacement of an entry by ∗∉F*\not\in F∗∈F.

Error: substitution of one entry by a different one.

Example: If dH(C)=dd_H(\mathcal{C})=ddH​(C)=d.

Error detection

Theorem: If dH(C)=dd_H(\mathcal{C})=ddH​(C)=d, then there exists f:Fn→C∩{"error detected"}f:F^n\to \mathcal{C}\cap \{\text{"error detected"}\}f:Fn→C∩{"error detected"}. that detects every patter of ≤d−1\leq d-1≤d−1 errors correctly.

  • That is, we can identify if the channel introduced at most d−1d-1d−1 errors.
  • No decoding is needed.

Idea:

Since dH(C)=dd_H(\mathcal{C})=ddH​(C)=d, one needs ≥d\geq d≥d errors to cause “confusion”.

Proof

The function

f(y)={y if y∈C"error detected"otherwisef(y)=\begin{cases} y\text{ if }y\in \mathcal{C}\\ \text{"error detected"} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}f(y)={y if y∈C"error detected"​otherwise​

will only fails if there are ≥d\geq d≥d errors.

Erasure correction

Theorem: If dH(C)=dd_H(\mathcal{C})=ddH​(C)=d, then there exists f:{Fn∪{∗}}→C∩{"failed"}f:\{F^n\cup \{*\}\}\to \mathcal{C}\cap \{\text{"failed"}\}f:{Fn∪{∗}}→C∩{"failed"}. that recovers every patter of at most d−1d-1d−1 erasures.

Idea:

Suppose d=4d=4d=4.

If 444 erasures occurred, there might be two possible codewords c,c′∈Cc,c'\in \mathcal{C}c,c′∈C.

If ≤3\leq 3≤3 erasures occurred, there is only one possible codeword c∈Cc\in \mathcal{C}c∈C.

Error correction

Define the Hamming ball of radius rrr centered at ccc as:

BH(c,r)={y∈Fn:dH(c,y)≤r}B_H(c,r)=\{y\in F^n:d_H(c,y)\leq r\}BH​(c,r)={y∈Fn:dH​(c,y)≤r}

Theorem: If dH(C)≥dd_H(\mathcal{C})\geq ddH​(C)≥d, then there exists f:Fn→Cf:F^n\to \mathcal{C}f:Fn→C that corrects every pattern of at most ⌊d−12⌋\lfloor \frac{d-1}{2}\rfloor⌊2d−1​⌋ errors.

Ideas:

The ball {BH(c,⌊d−12⌋)∣c∈C}\{B_H(c,\lfloor \frac{d-1}{2}\rfloor)|c\in \mathcal{C}\}{BH​(c,⌊2d−1​⌋)∣c∈C} are disjoint.

Use closest neighbor decoding, use triangle inequality.

Intro to linear codes

Summary: a code of minimum hamming distance ddd can

  • detect ≤d−1\leq d-1≤d−1 errors.
  • correct ≤d−1\leq d-1≤d−1 erasures.
  • Correct ≤⌊d−12⌋\leq \lfloor \frac{d-1}{2}\rfloor≤⌊2d−1​⌋ errors.

Problems:

  • How to construct good codes, k/nk/nk/n and ddd large?
  • How good can these codes possibly be?
  • How to encode?
  • How to decode with noisy channel

Tools

  • Linear algebra over finite fields.

Linear codes

Consider FnF^nFn as a vector space, and let C⊆Fn\mathcal{C}\subseteq F^nC⊆Fn be a subspace.

F,ΦF,\PhiF,Φ are finites, we use finite fields (algebraic objects that “immitate” Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, Cn\mathbb{C}^nCn).

Formally, satisfy the field axioms.

Next Lectures:

  • Field axioms
  • Prime fields (Fp\mathbb{F}_pFp​)
  • Field extensions (e.g. Fpt\mathbb{F}_{p^t}Fpt​)
